text: The laparoscopic view shows normal anatomy. A brisket attractor is placed in the vagina, and dissection is performed into the rectovaginal space up to the rectovaginal septum. The surgeon identifies the right ureter and sacral promontory, then incises the peritoneum over the sacral promontory, extending the incision towards the rectovaginal space.

The surgeon performs dissection into the presacral space, exposing the anterior longitudinal ligament of the sacrum. A 4x17 cm strip of polypropylene mesh is introduced and sutured to the iliococcygeus muscle bilaterally using two OPDS sutures.
On the contralateral side, the surgeon places an EEA sizer in the rectum and sutures the mesh to the anterior rectal wall using four interrupted sutures of 2-0 PDS.

The surgeon uses an EEA sizer to delineate the rectal wall, with a ten millimeter camera port at the umbilicus, and additional ports on the right and left sides for instrument access. The eight millimeter port is used for introducing sutures and tying knots extracorporeally with a closed end knot pusher. Dissection proceeds into the vesicle vaginal space, with the bladder backfilled with 200 cc of water to define its edge. A second strip of 4x12 cm polypropylene mesh is introduced into the abdominal cavity and sutured to the anterior vaginal wall using five to six interrupted sutures of 2-0 PDS.

The surgeon tensions two strips of polypropylene mesh against the anterior longitudinal ligament of the sacrum and sutures them in place using two zero-proline sutures. Excess mesh is trimmed before placing the second stitch.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal incision using a 2-0 monocryl suture in a purse-string fashion, effectively retroperitonealizing the mesh.
